one_line_takeaway: CryMisTa uses an LLM prompt-engineering pipeline (identification -> classification -> hierarchical construction) to automatically grow a cryptographic-API-misuse taxonomy from real code, producing 279 base categories (36 novel, expert-validated) from 3,492 programs with GPT-4, and shows 11 of those novel categories translate into working detection rules that catch misuses existing tools (CryScanner, CogniCryptSAST) previously missed.
key_finding: On CAM presence detection (Table 2, program-level, n=716): GPT-4 F1=0.944 (Accuracy 0.91 = 648/716, Precision 0.91 = 527/582, Recall 0.98 = 527/540); Claude-3.5 F1=0.940 (Accuracy 0.90 = 647/716, Precision 0.92 = 518/565, Recall 0.96 = 518/540); Gemini-1.5 F1=0.935 (Accuracy 0.90 = 644/716, Precision 0.95 = 496/526, Recall 0.92 = 497/540); Llama-3 F1=0.924 (Accuracy 0.88 = 631/716, Precision 0.89 = 521/587, Recall 0.96 = 521/540); GPT-3.5 F1=0.790 (Accuracy 0.78 = 560/716, Precision 0.98 = 392/400, Recall 0.73 = 392/540) and was dropped from subsequent experiments for its clearly worse performance. On instance-count completeness (Dsub, 975 ground-truth instances): GPT-4 reported 1,470 instances, Llama-3 1,544, Gemini-1.5 936, Claude-3.5 1,654; manual mapping of GPT-4's output against ground truth gave a False Negative Rate of 11.0% (107/975 missed) and a False Discovery Rate of 1.6% (24/1,470 false positives). On categorization behavior over 1,735 fixed summaries, GPT-4 and Claude-3.5 produced the fewest base categories (44 and 58 respectively, slowest growth), Llama-3 the most (315, fastest growth), with Gemini-1.5/GPT-3.5 intermediate. The final GPT-4-guided taxonomy over full corpus D has 8 top-level, 23 second-level, and 279 base categories, reproducible across 5 differently-ordered reruns. Taxonomy comparison (Table 3): CryMisTa's base categories fully cover CryptoRex (6/6), CryptoGo (12/12), and CryptoAPI-Bench (16/16); against MASC (105 categories) it overlaps on 93, misses 12 (10 of which had no corresponding code samples in MASC's own open dataset, and 2 are Android-specific categories excluded because D contained no Android app code); CryMisTa additionally surfaces 36 base categories absent from all five compared taxonomies (85 vs CryptoRex, 44 vs CryptoGo, 36 vs CryptoAPI-Bench, 36 vs MASC in the 'CryMisTa-X' column), externally validated by cryptography experts as legitimate misuse types (e.g., outdated Keccak256 padding via sha3.NewLegacyKeccak256 instead of NIST FIPS 202 sha3.New256, inappropriate GCM tag length per NIST SP 800-38D, insecure XTS tweak calculation, nonce reuse in symmetric encryption, deprecated OpenSSL locking callbacks, insecure token handling per RFC 6750/NIST SP800-63, missing certificate pinning). For RQ3, 11 of the 36 novel categories were manually encoded as detection rules and, when integrated into CryScanner/CogniCryptSAST or run as standalone scripts, successfully detected all matching instances in D that the base tools previously missed (demonstrated concretely for a CrySL rule enforcing OPENSSL_init_crypto()/OPENSSL_cleanse() usage and forbidding OPENSSL_add_all_digests()).
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is CryMisTa, an LLM-agnostic prompt-engineering pipeline (1,317 lines of Python) that automatically constructs a hierarchical cryptographic API misuse (CAM) taxonomy from real-world source code, replacing the manual, human-expert-curated construction used by all prior CAM taxonomies. The pipeline has three phases: (1) Identification — a Direct Identification Loop (zero-shot) plus a Chain-of-Thought Identification Loop (for cases the zero-shot loop cannot resolve) that report each detected misuse as a structured triplet <abstract, detail, code>; (2) Classification — a Summarization Loop that groups instances into <title, explanation> CAM categories and a Merging Loop that deduplicates categories produced across batches; (3) Taxonomy Construction — a Construction Prompt that builds a baseline hierarchy from a keyword set (adapted from Ami et al.'s 9-category attribute list) and an Expansion Prompt that incrementally folds in new batches. The authors instantiated the prototype with gpt-4-turbo-preview (temperature 0, token limit 4,095) and additionally benchmarked GPT-3.5, Llama-3 (llama3.1-405b-instruct), Gemini-1.5-flash, and Claude-3.5-sonnet-20240620 under identical settings to quantitatively compare LLMs on CAM detection and classification quality. Running CryMisTa (with GPT-4) over a 3,492-program corpus produced a taxonomy with 8 top-level categories, 23 second-level categories, and 279 base categories, 36 of which were not present in five prior taxonomies (CryptoRex, CryptoGo, CryptoAPI-Bench, MASC, LICMA). The authors then validated practical utility by hand-encoding 11 of the 36 novel categories into detection rules (CrySL rules integrated into CryScanner/CogniCryptSAST, or standalone Python detection logic) and showed these rules newly detect misuse instances the underlying tools previously missed.
threat_model: Not an adversarial threat model in the traditional sense; the paper addresses a knowledge-completeness/coverage problem in defensive tooling. The 'threat' being mitigated is that cryptographic API misuse (CAM) — e.g., weak ciphers, hardcoded keys/secrets, insecure IVs/nonces, insufficient key lengths, disabled certificate/hostname verification — silently weakens real deployed cryptography (the paper cites a prior finding that 88% of Android apps contain at least one CAM instance) and that existing detection tools miss many misuse patterns because their rule sets were manually curated by humans and cannot keep pace with evolving practice. No adversary model against the LLM pipeline itself (e.g., prompt injection into analyzed source code, poisoning of the taxonomy) is considered or evaluated.
limitations: Detection completeness (false-negative/false-positive analysis) was only computed for GPT-4, the best-performing model — the authors explicitly state the same analysis for the other LLMs 'we leave for future work.' The authors note GPT-4's 107 missed instances stem from (a) prioritizing the primary misuse in a snippet and overlooking secondary ones (e.g., catching weak RC4 but missing a co-located hardcoded key), and (b) gaps in pretrained cryptographic knowledge (e.g., missing insecure CFB8 mode or CAST5 algorithm); false positives clustered around 'hardcoded cryptographic algorithm names' and 'insecure secret lengths,' attributed to GPT-4 relying on general knowledge rather than deep API-usage analysis. The taxonomy keyword set for classification currently reuses Ami et al.'s root-cause attribute keywords, and the paper notes future work is needed on how the choice of classification keywords affects taxonomy utility. The token limit (4,095) constrains input code segment size, requiring static-analysis-based code slicing; the paper flags overcoming LLM token limits for complex projects as future work. The 12 MASC categories the taxonomy misses partly reflect corpus composition (no Android app code in D) rather than a pipeline limitation. Only 11 of 36 novel categories were validated end-to-end as detection rules; the rest were not operationalized in this paper. Reproducibility of the taxonomy shape was tested only for input-order variation (5 reruns), not for other sources of LLM nondeterminism or model-version drift.

datasets_used: D: 3,492 real-world programs (C/C++, Java, Python, Go) — 457 from prior benchmarks (18 CryptoGo, 196 CryptoAPI-Bench, 40 MASC, 203 LICMA) + 3,035 crawled from GitHub
Dsub: 716-program manually labeled ground-truth subset (245 Python, 231 Java, 120 C/C++, 120 Go; 540 CAM-containing / 176 clean; 975 labeled CAM instances)
quantitative_results: CAM-presence detection F1 (Table 2): GPT-4 0.944, Claude-3.5 0.940, Gemini-1.5 0.935, Llama-3 0.924, GPT-3.5 0.790
GPT-4 detection completeness on Dsub: False Negative Rate 11.0% (107/975 missed), False Discovery Rate 1.6% (24/1,470 false positives)
Reported CAM instance counts on Dsub: GPT-4 1,470; Llama-3 1,544; Gemini-1.5 936; Claude-3.5 1,654
Base categories induced from 1,735 fixed summaries: GPT-4 44, Claude-3.5 58, Gemini-1.5/GPT-3.5 moderate, Llama-3 315 (highest)
Final GPT-4-guided taxonomy over full corpus D: 8 top-level categories, 23 second-level categories, 279 base categories; identical (279/8) across 5 reruns with shuffled input order

Taxonomy overlap (Table 3): fully covers CryptoRex (6/6), CryptoGo (12/12), CryptoAPI-Bench (16/16); overlaps 93/105 MASC categories, misses 12 (10 with no code samples in MASC's dataset, 2 Android-specific)

36 base categories in CryMisTa's taxonomy absent from all 5 compared taxonomies; 11 of these were encoded into detection rules and successfully detected previously-missed instances in D when integrated into CryScanner/CogniCryptSAST
baselines_compared: LLMs: GPT-4 (gpt-4-turbo-preview), GPT-3.5 (gpt-3.5-turbo-preview), Llama-3 (llama3.1-405b-instruct), Gemini-1.5 (gemini-1.5-flash), Claude-3.5 (claude-3.5-sonnet-20240620)

Existing CAM taxonomies/tools: CryptoRex (6 categories), CryptoGo (12), CryptoAPI-Bench (16), MASC (105), LICMA; detection tools CryScanner and CogniCryptSAST used as integration targets for validating new rules
